volumeTitle: Amphitryon. The Comedy of Asses. The Pot of Gold. The Two Bacchises. The Captives
label: The Comedy of Asses (Section 5.1.846b - Section 5.2.909)

sections.label: Section 5.1.846b
sections.original: Ergo sunt quae exoptas : mihi quae ego exoptem volo .
sections.translation: Then you have what you desire ; for myself , I wish for what I could desire .

sections.label: Section 5.1.847
sections.original: Vnum hunc diem perpetere , quoniam tibi potestatem dedi ,
sections.translation: Submit to it this one day , since I ’ ve given you the power to be with her for a year , and have procured for you the command of money in your amour .

sections.label: Section 5.1.849_850
sections.original: Em istoc me facto tibi devinxti .
sections.translation: Well , by doing that , you have laid me under an obligation to you .

sections.label: Section 5.1.849_850b
sections.original: Quin te ergo hilarum das mihi ?
sections.translation: Why then don ’ t you show yourself cheerful to me ?

sections.label: Section 5.2.851
sections.original: Áin tu meum virum híc potare , óbsecro , cum fílio
sections.translation: Prithee , do you say that my husband is carousing here , together with my son ?— and that he has carried to his mistress twenty minae of silver ?— and that , with the knowledge of my son , his father is perpetrating this wickedness ?

sections.label: Section 5.2.854
sections.original: Neque divini neque mi humani posthac quicquam accreduas ,
sections.translation: Trust me in nothing henceforth , either divine or human ,

sections.label: Section 5.2.855
sections.original: Artemona , si huius rei mé mendacem inveneris .
sections.translation: Artemona , if you find me untruthful in this matter .

sections.label: Section 5.2.856
sections.original: At scelesta ego praeter alios meum virum † frugi rata ,
sections.translation: Wretched then am I , who have supposed that , beyond others , my husband was sober , decent , chaste , and especially fond of his wife .

sections.label: Section 5.2.858
sections.original: At nunc dehinc scito illum ante omnes minimi mortalem preti ,
sections.translation: But now , henceforth , understand that he , before all men , is a person of the smallest worth — a drunkard , a good-for-nothing fellow , unchaste , and a contemner of his own wife .

sections.label: Section 5.2.860
sections.original: Pol ni istaec vera essent , numquam faceret ea quae nunc facit .
sections.translation: I ’ faith , if this wasn ’ t true , he would never be doing the things that he is now doing .

sections.label: Section 5.2.861
sections.original: Ego quoque hercle illum antehac hominem semper sum frugi ratus ,
sections.translation: Upon my word , I too always hitherto took him to be a decent person ; but by this action he declares himself ,— to be carousing , indeed , together with his son , and , a decrepit old man , toying with a mistress in his company .

sections.label: Section 5.2.864
sections.original: Hoc ecastor est quod ille it ad cenam cottidie .
sections.translation: For this it is , i ’ faith , that he ’ s going out to dinner every day .

sections.label: Section 5.2.865
sections.original: ait sese ire ad Archidemum , Chaeream , Chaerestratum ,
sections.translation: He says that he ’ s going to Archidemus , Chereas , Chaerestratus , Clinius , Chremes , Cratinus , Dinias , Demosthenes : while he ’ s thinking of debauchery , and public dens of infamy with his harlot .

sections.label: Section 5.2.868
sections.original: Quin tu illum iubes ancillas rapere sublimen domum ?
sections.translation: Why don ’ t you bid your maid-servants carry him off home upon their shoulders ?

sections.label: Section 5.2.869
sections.original: Tace modo . ne ego illum ecastor miserum habebo .
sections.translation: Do you only be quiet . By my troth , I ’ ll surely give him some trouble .

sections.label: Section 5.2.869b
sections.original: Ego istúc scio ,
sections.translation: I ’ m sure of it , that so it will befall him , so long , indeed , as you shall continue married to him .

sections.label: Section 5.2.870b
sections.original: Ego censeo .
sections.translation: I was fancying that this fellow was still giving attendance either in the senate or to his dependants : that for that reason it was , that , worn out with fatigue , he snored the whole night through . Wearied with his labours out of doors he comes home at night . Another ’ s farm he ploughs , his own he leaves untilled .

sections.label: Section 5.2.875
sections.original: is etiam corruptus porro suom corrumpit filium .
sections.translation: Both he himself is corrupted , and he corrupts his son as well .

sections.label: Section 5.2.876
sections.original: Sequere hac me modó , iam faxo ipsum hominem manifesto opprimas .
sections.translation: Do you only follow me this way , I ’ ll make you just now to fall upon the man himself in the very fact .

sections.label: Section 5.2.877
sections.original: Nihil ecastor est quod facere mavelim .
sections.translation: By heaven , there ’ s nothing that I could more wish for .

sections.label: Section 5.2.877b
sections.original: Mane dum .
sections.translation: Just stop there .

sections.label: Section 5.2.877c
sections.original: Quid est ?
sections.translation: What ’ s the matter ?

sections.label: Section 5.2.878
sections.original: Possis , si forte accubantem tuom virum conspexeris
sections.translation: If perchance you were to see your husband reclining , if you beheld him with a garland on , caressing a mistress , could you recognize him ?

sections.label: Section 5.2.880
sections.original: Possum ecastor .
sections.translation: I ’ troth , I could .

sections.label: Section 5.2.880b
sections.original: Em tibi hominem .
sections.translation: Then there ’ s your man .

sections.label: Section 5.2.880c
sections.original: Perii .
sections.translation: I ’ m undone .

sections.label: Section 5.2.880d
sections.original: Paulisper mane .
sections.translation: Stay a little . Let ’ s observe in private , from ambush , what business they are about .

sections.label: Section 5.2.882
sections.original: Quid modi , pater , amplexando facies ?
sections.translation: What end will you put to your caresses , father ?

sections.label: Section 5.2.882b
sections.original: Fateor , gnate mi —
sections.translation: I confess , my son —

sections.label: Section 5.2.883
sections.original: Quid fatere ?
sections.translation: What do you confess ?

sections.label: Section 5.2.883b
sections.original: Me ex amore húius corruptum oppido .
sections.translation: That I ’ m utterly undone with love for her .

sections.label: Section 5.2.884
sections.original: Audin quid ait ?
sections.translation: Do you hear what he says ?

sections.label: Section 5.2.884b
sections.original: Audio .
sections.translation: I hear .

sections.label: Section 5.2.884c
sections.original: Egon ut non domo uxori meae
sections.translation: Ought I not to filch the mantle from my wife at home ,

sections.label: Section 5.2.885
sections.original: subripiam in deliciis pallam quam hábet , atque ad te deferam ,
sections.translation: which she is so fond of , and bring it to you ? Though my wife ’ s life should last a whole year in consequence , by my troth I could not be dissuaded from doing so .

sections.label: Section 5.2.887
sections.original: Censen tu illum hodié primum ire ádsuetum esse in ganeum ?
sections.translation: Do you suppose that he has been accustomed to frequent a brothel to-day for the first time ?

sections.label: Section 5.2.888
sections.original: Ille ecastor suppilabat me , quod ancillas meas
sections.translation: Upon my faith , it was he that was pilfering me ; whereas I was suspecting my maid-servants , and was tormenting the wretched creatures who were innocent all the while .

sections.label: Section 5.2.889b
sections.original: Pater ,
sections.translation: Father ,

sections.label: Section 5.2.890
sections.original: iube dari vinum ; iam dudum factum est cum primum bibi .
sections.translation: bid him pour out some wine ; ’ tis a long time since I drank first [*] .

sections.label: Section 5.2.891
sections.original: Da , puere , ab summo . áge tu interibi ab infimo da savium .
sections.translation: Begin , boy , from the top [*] . ( To PHILENIUM .) Come , do you meanwhile from below give me a kiss .

sections.label: Section 5.2.892
sections.original: Perii misera , ut osculatur carnufex , capuli decus .
sections.translation: Wretch that I am , I ’ m undone ! How the villain , the garnishing of a bier [*] , is kissing away .

sections.label: Section 5.2.893
sections.original: Edepol animam suaviorem aliquanto quam uxoris meae .
sections.translation: A breath , by my faith , somewhat sweeter than that of my wife .

sections.label: Section 5.2.894
sections.original: Dic amabo , an fetet anima úxoris tuae ?
sections.translation: Tell me , there ’ s a dear , does the breath of your wife smell bad ?

sections.label: Section 5.2.894b
sections.original: Nauteam
sections.translation: I ’ d prefer to drink bilge-water , if it were necessary , rather than kiss her .

sections.label: Section 5.2.896
sections.original: Ain tandem ? edepol ne tu istuc cum malo magno tuo
sections.translation: Aye , pray , how say you ? By my troth , to your own great detriment , you ’ ve surely said that against me . Never mind ; only de you come home , I ’ ll let you know what danger there is in speaking abusively against a wife with a dowry .

sections.label: Section 5.2.899
sections.original: Miser ecastor es .
sections.translation: By heavens , you are a wretch .

sections.label: Section 5.2.899b
sections.original: Ecastor dignus est .
sections.translation: I ’ faith , he ’ s deserving so to be .

sections.label: Section 5.2.899c
sections.original: Quid ais , pater ?
sections.translation: How say you , father ?

sections.label: Section 5.2.900
sections.original: ecquid matrem amas ?
sections.translation: Don ’ t you love my mother ?

sections.label: Section 5.2.900b
sections.original: Egone illam ? nunc amo , quia non adest .
sections.translation: Who — I ? I love her just now , because she isn ’ t present .

sections.label: Section 5.2.901
sections.original: Quid cum adest ?
sections.translation: How , when she is present ?

sections.label: Section 5.2.901b
sections.original: Periisse cupio .
sections.translation: Then , I wish she was dead .

sections.label: Section 5.2.901c
sections.original: Amat homo hic te , ut praedicat .
sections.translation: This fellow is fond of you , according to what he says .

sections.label: Section 5.2.902
sections.original: Ne illa ecastor faenerato funditat : nam si domum
sections.translation: On my word , he ’ s surely laying out all this at interest ; for if , this day , he returns home , I ’ ll especially have my revenge in kissing him .

sections.label: Section 5.2.904
sections.original: Iace , pater , talos , ut porro nos iaciamus .
sections.translation: Father , throw the dice ; that afterwards I may have a throw .

sections.label: Section 5.2.904b
sections.original: Maxime .
sections.translation: By all means .

sections.label: Section 5.2.905
sections.original: te , Philaenium , mihi atque uxoris mortem . hoc Venerium est .
sections.translation: You , Philenium , for myself , and death for my wife . ( He throws .) ’ Tis Venus ’ s cast [*] . Lads , clap your hands , and give me some honeyed wine in my cup , in honor of my throw .

sections.label: Section 5.2.907
sections.original: Non queo durare .
sections.translation: I can hold out no longer .

sections.label: Section 5.2.907b
sections.original: Si non didicisti fulloniam ,
sections.translation: If you haven ’ t learned the fulling trade [*] , it ’ s not to be wondered at ; now it ’ s quite fitting for you to beset his eyes . ( They make their appearance before the revellers .)

sections.label: Section 5.2.909
sections.original: Ego pol vivam et tu istaec hodie cum tuo magno malo
sections.translation: By heavens , I will live , and you this day have made that invocation to your own great misfortune .
